perturbation.original_text: (e) This Agreement will be governed and interpreted according to the laws of the State of California, without reference to principles of conflicts of laws. Each party hereto expressly consents to the personal jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in Santa Clara County, California, and expressly waives any defense to any action based on inconvenient forum, choice of venue, lack of personal jurisdiction, sufficiency of service of process or the like.
perturbation.changed_text: (e) This Agreement will be governed and interpreted according to the laws of the State of California, without reference to principles of conflicts of laws. In the event of any dispute, the parties agree to resolve the conflict within the jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in Guam. Each party hereto expressly consents to the personal jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in Santa Clara County, California, and expressly waives any defense to any action based on inconvenient forum, choice of venue, lack of personal jurisdiction, sufficiency of service of process or the like.
perturbation.explanation: The jurisdiction clause is being modified to create a contradiction. It initially states that disputes will be resolved in California courts, but then inserts a contradictory sentence stating disputes will be resolved within the jurisdiction of courts in Guam. This introduces uncertainty about the governing jurisdiction, weakening the enforceability of the clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 dictates the proper venue for civil actions in federal courts. By creating conflicting statements regarding the venue (California vs. Guam), the modified text contradicts the clarity and predictability required by this law, potentially leading to disputes over the correct venue.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Applicability of Section.— Except as otherwise provided by law— (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General.— A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located; (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated; or (3) if there is no district in which an action may otherwise be brought as provided in this section, any judicial district in which any defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to such action. (c) Residency.— For all venue purposes— (1) a natural person, including an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States, shall be deemed to reside in the judicial district in which that person is domiciled; (2) an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued in its common name under applicable law, whether or not incorporated, shall be deemed to reside, if a defendant, in any judicial district in which such defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question and, if a plaintiff, only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business; and (3) a defendant not resident in the United States may be sued in any judicial district, and the joinder of such a defendant shall be disregarded in determining where the action may be brought w
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces conflicting venue clauses, initially specifying Santa Clara County, California, then adding Guam. This creates a direct contradiction regarding where disputes will be resolved. The original text clearly designates California, while the changed text introduces uncertainty by adding Guam as a possible venue. This uncertainty undermines the enforceability of the venue clause, as it is unclear which jurisdiction would have authority. 28 U.S. Code § 1391, as indicated in both provided snippets, governs venue in federal courts, aiming to provide clarity and predictability. The conflicting statements in the modified text directly contradict this purpose.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any litigation or arbitral proceeding between they parties regarding this Agreement, the advertising materials or the obligations of the parties hereunder, the party not prevailing therein shall pay the reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs of the party prevailing therein.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any litigation or arbitral proceeding between they parties regarding this Agreement, the advertising materials or the obligations of the parties hereunder, the parties shall each bear their own attorneys' fees and court costs, irrespective of the outcome.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates that the losing party pays the attorneys' fees and court costs of the prevailing party. The modified clause stipulates that each party bears their own costs, regardless of who wins. This change could deter parties from pursuing legitimate claims, as they would bear their own legal expenses even if successful.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021 generally allows parties to contractually agree on who pays attorneys' fees. While the modified text doesn't directly violate the statute, it can be seen as undermining the intent of allowing parties to allocate risk and potentially deterring litigation of valid claims, contradicting the spirit of fair access to justice which informs the legal framework around attorneys' fees.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021. Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 377, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original agreement regarding attorney's fees and court costs. The original text states the losing party shall pay, while the changed text states each party bears their own costs. While CCP 1021 allows parties to agree on attorney's fees, the change fundamentally alters the risk allocation agreed upon in the original contract. By stipulating that each party bears their own costs 'irrespective of the outcome,' the changed text removes a previously agreed upon financial responsibility, therefore, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Either party's failure or delay in exercising any of its rights will not constitute a waiver of such rights unless expressly waived in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party's failure or delay in exercising any of its rights will constitute a waiver of such rights.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states that a failure to exercise a right does not constitute a waiver unless expressly waived in writing. The modified version removes the condition 'unless expressly waived in writing', so a simple delay or failure can now be interpreted as a waiver of that right. This introduces ambiguity and makes it easier to argue that a party has waived its rights through inaction, which can undermine the enforceability of certain contract provisions.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3513 allows anyone to waive the benefit of a law intended solely for their benefit. The original clause reinforces the requirement of an explicit, written waiver. The modified clause removes this explicit requirement, increasing the possibility of unintended waivers. This directly contradicts the intent of ensuring a knowing and voluntary waiver of a benefit, thus undermining the protections offered by this code.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3513. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 4. MAXIMS OF JURISPRUDENCE [3509 - 3548] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) 3513. Any one may waive the advantage of a law intended solely for their benefit. But a law established for a public reason cannot be contravened by a private agreement. (Amended by Stats. 2024, Ch. 812, Sec. 2. (AB 1899) Effective January 1, 2025.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts California Civil Code § 3513. The original text requires explicit written waivers, reinforcing the intent of a knowing and voluntary waiver. The changed text eliminates this requirement, increasing the risk of unintended waivers. Snippet 1 states: "Any one may waive the advantage of a law intended solely for their benefit." By removing the explicit written requirement, the modified text makes it easier for a party to unintentionally waive their rights, undermining the protection intended by the law. Therefore, the change creates a legal contradiction.
